blurb: The iconic scrub oak of the Front Range foothills — deeply lobed leaves turning gold and russet in fall, acorns that feed everything, and a tough, thicket-forming nature that thrives on dry slopes.
size: ~15–30 ft tall × 15–25 ft wide (often a multi-stem thicket)
seasons: Deeply lobed green leaves turning gold-russet in fall; acorns late summer–fall; bare gray branches in winter
wildlife: Acorns and cover are a wildlife powerhouse — deer, turkeys, jays, squirrels; larval host for many butterflies and moths
deer: Browsed, and acorns eaten — a key wildlife plant
gaps: No clean open-licensed acorn close-up found; acorns are described but the reel shows foliage, habit, and fall color.

care.hardiness: Very cold-hardy — rated USDA zones 3–9 and well-adapted at zone 5, so it is bulletproof through Boulder's 5b–6a winters.[1]
care.planting: Plant container-grown nursery stock in spring or early fall; native trees transplant poorly because of a deep root system, so set out small containerized plants rather than digging wild ones, and disturb the roots as little as possible.[1] Container plants dry fast in our climate and need watering through establishment; in the ground, water during the first season, then taper off.[1]
care.sun: Full sun is best on the Front Range; continuous shade reduces growth and form.[1]
care.soil: Thrives in rocky, lean, alkaline soils — a natural fit for Boulder's high-pH clay — and tolerates everything from sandy loam to clay.[1][2]
care.water: Once established it is drought tolerant and needs no irrigation, matching our ~15 in of annual precipitation.[1] Water new plants to soak the soil 10–15 in deep every 3–4 weeks through establishment and in unusually dry years, then leave it dry.[1]
care.spacing: Forms a large shrub or small tree averaging 20–30 ft tall and ~15 ft wide, often spreading into clonal thickets — give it room and expect it to sucker.[1]
care.propagation: From seed: collect brown acorns in fall and sow immediately (they lose viability fast in storage); a short 2-week cold-moist stratification at ~36°F (2°C) maximizes germination, and acorns can be soaked in hot water ~15 min to kill weevils before sowing.[3][4] Sow into deep containers to accommodate the long initial taproot. Stem cuttings rarely succeed; it spreads naturally by rhizomes/lignotubers, and layering works only ~13–21% of the time.[1][3]
care.pruning: Prune in the dormant season — late fall through late winter, before spring growth — thinning stems and removing dead or damaged wood, which in a dense stand also cuts the fire fuel.[1] Do NOT prune oaks from April to October: fresh growing-season wounds draw the beetles and borers that spread oak disease, so hold cuts to the dormant window (and if a limb must come off in summer, seal the wound to mask the cut).[5] Gambel oak suckers into clonal thickets, so remove root sprouts at the base if you want to hold it as a single small tree rather than a spreading motte.[1][3]
care.maintenance: Protect the roots from construction damage, which is a leading cause of decline; otherwise low-care.[1]
care.troubles: Dense oak thickets are a wildfire fuel; few serious pest or disease problems otherwise.[1]

edible.summary: Acorns are a traditional food — but only after the bitter tannins are leached out; raw acorns are astringent and cause stomach upset.[2][4]
edible.parts: The acorn kernels (nutmeats), gathered ripe and brown in fall.[2] They were a staple food of Native American peoples across the oak's range.[2]
edible.preparation: Shell the acorns, then leach out the bitter tannic acid: boil the kernels in repeated changes of water (about 15 min per change) until the water no longer turns brown — roughly two hours total — then roast or grind them.[4]
edible.caution: Do NOT eat raw acorns. All parts of Gambel oak contain gallotannins (juvenile foliage and acorns highest), which are bitter, astringent, and cause GI upset in quantity; the same tannins poison livestock that eat oak as more than ~50% of their diet.[2][3] Only the leached, cooked kernels are safe to eat.[4]
